title: The Symposium
subtitle: Συμπόσιον (Symposion) — On Love, Desire, and the Ascent to the Beautiful
author: Plato

recommendedTranslation.title: The Symposium
recommendedTranslation.note: Lively modern translation with an excellent introduction, detailed notes, and a select bibliography. The most accessible current Penguin edition for first-time readers.

alternativeTranslations.note: A crisp, idiomatic translation widely used in undergraduate philosophy courses. Includes an interpretive essay and a glossary of key terms.
Oxford edition with a substantial introduction situating the dialogue in its historical and philosophical context. Strong scholarly apparatus.
Unusual, literal translation that preserves Plato's Greek word order and syntax. Includes Benardete's interpretive essay. Favored by Straussians and close readers.

alternativeTranslations.publisher: Loeb Classical Library / Harvard University Press
alternativeTranslations.note: Standard bilingual edition (Greek/English). Includes the complete Symposium with facing-page translation. The definitive scholarly reference text.

themes: Diotima of Mantinea and the Priestess Tradition
Socratic Erotics and the Desire for Wisdom
Alcibiades and the Problem of Embodied Love
Phaedrus on Love and Heroic Sacrifice
Pausanias on Heavenly vs. Common Love
The Relationship Between Love and Immortality
Comedy and Tragedy in Philosophical Discourse
excerpt: Plato's Symposium is the foundational text of Western philosophical reflection on love. Set at a drinking party in Athens in 416 BC, the dialogue presents seven encomia to the god Eros delivered by some of the most remarkable figures of the age: the orator Phaedrus, the legal expert Pausanias, the physician Eryximachus, the comic playwright Aristophanes, the tragic poet Agathon, the philosopher Socrates, and finally the drunken general Alcibiades. Through the voice of the wise woman Diotima of Mantinea, Socrates articulates the 'Ladder of Love' — an ascent from desire for a single beautiful body to contemplation of the Form of Beauty itself. The Symposium is simultaneously a literary masterpiece, a profound ethical inquiry, and the dialogue in which Plato first unequivocally introduces the Theory of Forms. Its influence radiates through Plotinus, Augustine, Ficino, the Renaissance love treatise tradition, and every subsequent thinker who has tried to understand what it means to love.
